title: Shortcut-error signatures in coherence-retaining endpoint work quasistatistics
authors: Gabriella G. Damas, G. D. de Moraes Neto
abstract: Quantum work statistics differ from classical ones because initial energy coherence matters. The standard two-point measurement (TPM) gives a positive distribution but erases phase information. Coherence-retaining endpoint-work quasistatistics provide a compact probe of shortcut-to-adiabaticity performance. For work defined with respect to a reference Hamiltonian, an exact counterdiabatic shortcut pulls the final reference Hamiltonian back to an operator diagonal in the initial energy basis. Endpoint Kirkwood-Dirac or Margenau-Hill quasistatistics then lose sensitivity to initial coherence and reduce to the TPM result. Imperfect shortcuts restore this sensitivity: a non-commuting control error produces off-diagonal pulled-back Hamiltonian elements at first order in the error amplitude, whereas population-only transition probabilities change only at second order. Harmonic-oscillator and qubit benchmarks confirm this linear-versus-quadratic contrast. The result complements inclusive work-cost analyses: it does not measure the auxiliary field's energetic cost, but provides a phase-sensitive endpoint diagnostic of residual nonadiabaticity.
summary: The paper introduces a new way to detect errors in fast quantum control protocols by using work statistics that retain initial phase information. It proves that these 'coherent' statistics respond linearly to errors, making them much more sensitive than traditional methods that only see quadratic changes in populations. This provides a powerful new diagnostic tool for calibrating shortcuts to adiabaticity.
main_problem: Developing a sensitive diagnostic to detect and calibrate non-commuting errors in Shortcuts to Adiabaticity (STA) protocols, which traditional population-based measurements fail to detect efficiently.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that coherence-retaining endpoint work statistics provide a first-order (linear) signal of non-commuting errors, whereas traditional population-transfer diagnostics only scale quadratically with the error amplitude.
method: The study uses perturbation theory and quasiprobability frameworks (Kirkwood-Dirac and Margenau-Hill) to compare the scaling of coherent energy signals against population-based metrics.
model_or_system: The theoretical framework is applied to closed-system unitary dynamics, specifically using a qubit and a time-dependent harmonic oscillator as benchmark models for STA protocols.
key_observables: Coherent endpoint work signal (delta W_coh), population excess (Q*^-1), and Kirkwood-Dirac/Margenau-Hill quasiprobability weights.
important_parameters: Error amplitude (epsilon), error generator (K), initial state phase (theta), and dephasing rate (Gamma).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes closed-system unitary dynamics and focuses on the small-error regime where the linear-versus-quadratic hierarchy is preserved.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the workflow between TPM and coherence-retaining branches; Figure 2 demonstrates the linear vs. quadratic scaling in the oscillator; Figure 5 shows the impact of dephasing on signal contrast; Figure 6 verifies the scaling for waveform distortions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of coherence erasure in TPM, proposes a coherence-retaining diagnostic, provides analytical scaling proofs, validates the method with qubit and oscillator benchmarks, and tests robustness against dephasing and waveform errors.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and quantum control, as it provides a more sensitive, low-overhead method for detecting subtle non-adiabatic errors in fast control protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Endpoint-work diagnostic flowchart. The tradi- tional TPM branch applies an initial dephasing map ∆0, eras- ing energy-basis coherences. The Kirkwood-Dirac (KD) and Margenau-Hill (MH) branch retains the full initial state ρ0. The difference between their endpoint means, ∆Wcoh, isolates the nonadiabatic coherent response driven by the off-diagonal pulled-back Hamiltonian Hoff.
FIG. 2. Oscillator benchmark. (a) Coherent endpoint-work correction ∆Wcoh/ωf versus relative phase θ and missing-CD amplitude ϵ for the probe |ψθ⟩= (|0⟩+ eiθ|2⟩)/ √

FIG. 3. Oscillator endpoint-error fingerprints. (a) Protocol- level diagnostics: residual Bogoliubov amplitude |ν|, pop- ulation excess Q∗−1, and phase-optimized coherent sig- nal maxθ |∆Wcoh|/ωf for exact STA, imperfect STA, and a bare ramp. (b) Band-resolved off-diagonal weight B∆n = P

FIG. 4. Qubit benchmark of endpoint quasistatistics. (a) Transverse endpoint field 1 2|hH,⊥| and transition prob- ability Ptr versus missing-CD amplitude ϵ, showing linear and quadratic scaling, respectively. (b) Phase-optimized quasiprobability witnesses Dmax KD and N max MH versus ϵ. (c) Characteristic-function gap |χρ(u) −χ∆ρ(u)| for exact STA and a 5% missing-CD error; the exact-STA curve is limited by the numerical floor. (d–f) KD/MH matrix fingerprints: (d) real KD weights for exact STA; (e) real KD/MH weights for a 5% error at the phase maximizing NMH; and (f) imag- inary KD weights for a 5% error at the phase maximizing P

Figure 4(b) demonstrates that the quasiprobabilistic witnesses revive with the same perturbative hierarchy. Both Dmax KD and N max MH scale linearly with the shortcut im- perfection. Thus the first-order response is not restricted to the endpoint mean; it also appears in the KD/MH quasiprobability structure. The characteristic-function collapse predicted by Eq. 10 is verified in Fig. 4(c): the gap |χρ(u)−χ∆ρ(u)| vanishes for exact STA up to the nu- merical floor, but becomes finite under a 5% missing-CD error.
